raw_case:
Targetoid Hemosiderotic Hemangioma With Spontaneous Remission and Recurrence in a Male Patient: A Case Report
Targetoid hemosiderotic hemangioma is a rare, characteristic, benign lymphovascular lesion that usually affects the trunk and lower limbs. It has a diverse clinical appearance. Most affected people are in their 20s. A violaceous solitary papule encircled by a pale, narrow region and an ecchymotic ring peripherally, mimicking a target, is the most prevalent clinical characteristic. In many cases, the reported dermoscopic signs and patterns of hemosiderotic hemangioma have been found to be adequate for establishing a clinical diagnosis. The following is a case of a 23-year-old male presenting with blue-black vascular lesions on the lateral aspect of the right deltoid with a red ecchymotic ring. The 2×1 cm-sized lesion has a history of spontaneous remission and reappearance. Based on dermoscopic findings, a diagnosis of targetoid hemosiderotic hemangioma was made.

Introduction

All vascular tumors with hobnail-like endothelial cells on histology, whether benign or malignant, are collectively referred to as hobnail hemangiomas. This group includes retiform hemangioendothelioma, polymorphic hemangioendothelioma, progressive lymphangioma, skin or soft tissue angiosarcoma, Dabska tumor (malignant endovascular papillary angioendothelioma), and unusual vascular lesions following radiotherapy. Targetoid hemosiderotic hemangioma (THH) was initially identified by Aronberg and Santa Cruz in the 1980s as a hobnail hemangioma. Its histology and morphology differ according to the evolutionary stage [1]. Usually, it is seen in the limbs or trunk. It can manifest at any age between 5 and 67 years old, with younger age groups experiencing it more frequently. There is no preference for either gender [2]. Trauma to an underlying hemangioma and hormonal factors, as demonstrated by alterations in the lesions in women during menstruation, account for the pathophysiology of THH [3]. Hobnail hemangioma is another name for THH, a benign vascular tumor with a targetoid form. The clinical lesion known as THH appears as a tiny violaceous papule encircled by a peripheral ecchymotic ring and a pale, thin region. Mostly, such lesions present with a diameter not exceeding more than 1 cm [4]. It is possible that the patient's presentation will not always be as distinctive as in this particular instance. Lesions may appear as a papule or angiomatous macule without a halo. This variety has been explained by several theories, including distinct stages in the lesion's genesis and devolution, hormonal effects, or trauma to an already-existing angioma. Spontaneous devolution is shown by pale regions within the purpuric ring [5].

Case presentation

A 23-year-old male from Uttar Pradesh presented to the outpatient department with an asymptomatic vascular lesion on the deltoid region of his right arm for the past eight years. The lesion was blue-black, surrounded by a red ecchymotic ring. The lesion measured 2x1 cm and presented with a history of spontaneous resolution along with reappearances after two to three months of resolution (Figure 1).There was no history of progression in dimension, structure, or color. Further, there was no history of similar lesions on any other body part. No aggravating or relieving factors were reported. There was no sign or symptom suggestive of a history of pain, itching, or tenderness on or around the lesion. Malignant conditions were ruled out based on the lack of complaints consistent with the patient's history. The patient did not have a history of any chronic infections or addictions. There was no family history of skin lesions or any dermatological complaints. The patient was completely normal and could perform all activities expected out of a person of his age. A dermoscopic examination showed a central blue-black area, which is surrounded and demarcated by a red homogenous area with vascular structures. The central lesion is margined by red borders through its circumference (Figure 2).An excisional biopsy was performed on the patient for histopathological examination, which revealed dilated blood vessels lined by hobnail epithelial cells along with hemosiderin depositions in the deeper dermis (Figure 3).Based on presenting history, contact dermoscopy, and biopsy, the diagnosis of THH was made. The biopsy in itself was curative in addition to being a key factor in the establishment of a definitive diagnosis.

Discussion

Potential differential diagnoses include insect bite, dermatofibroma, infantile hemangioma, tufted angioma, melanocytic lesions, and Kaposi sarcoma, based on its current stage of evolution. The differential diagnosis includes early Kaposi's sarcoma, epithelioid hemangioma, progressive lymphangioma, and well-differentiated angiosarcoma based on presenting histology. In late lesions, fibrosis, profuse hemosiderin, and collapsed vascular lumina have all been documented [5]. Injury is proposed as one of the primary reasons for the targetoid appearance, even though the exact etiology of THH has not been determined. Trauma can cause the creation of microshunts, in which the capillary pressure causes erythrocytes to fill the lymph spaces within the lesion, hence encouraging the development of aneurysmal microstructures. If specific efferent lymphatic veins are occluded, there will be inflammation, fibrosis, and interstitial hemosiderin deposits [6].Histologically speaking, it is an abundance of uncircumscribed veins that may penetrate subcutaneous tissue. A superficial proliferation of ectatic dermal vascular lumina with intraluminal papillary projections seems to be the earliest discovery. Endothelial cells have strong intraluminal projections and are either flat or distinctly epithelioid. The angular, lymphatic-like lumina that cluster around sweat gland coils and frequently form tiny hemangiomatous nodules comprise the deeper component. Widespread fibrin thrombi, inflammatory aggregation, and red cell extravasation are seen [7]. Later phases show a considerable buildup of stromal hemosiderin. In endothelial cells, Ulex europaeus 1 lectin is considerably positive, whereas factor VIII-related antigen is just marginally positive. While self-limited, the lesion appears to be irreversible. Although it appears benign clinically, its histologic features are alarming [8]. The clinical-pathological connection serves as the foundation for diagnosis, particularly when it comes to the distinctive shape of the lesion. A straightforward excision can be used to make a correct histological diagnosis and is also therapeutic [9]. Since there is no evidence of local invasion or dissemination, such lesions are simply excised for diagnostic or cosmetological reasons due to the benign nature of the condition [10].

Conclusions

THH is a relatively uncommon benign disease that histologically resembles a malignant condition. Depending on the developmental stage at which the lesion was biopsied, histology varies. Early phases have a biphasic pattern: in the papillary dermis, tiny hemangiomatous nodules that split the collagen bundles are often formed by vascular spaces that are dilated and slit-like, resembling lymphatic veins that are concentrated near sweat glands. Similar to lymphatic vessels, the vascular compartments in the deep dermis are angulated, slit-like, have solid intraluminal projections, and resemble hobnails. Later stages reveal considerable hemosiderin buildup in the stroma with fibrosis in addition to a collapsed artery lumen. Despite its well-documented histology, it is unclear whether this tumor is caused by endothelial cells in blood arteries or lymphatic vessels. The diagnosis is based on a clinical-pathological connection, notably the lesion's unique appearance. Curative simple excision allows for an accurate histological diagnosis. There have been no confirmed recurrences since the lesion was removed. To ensure an early diagnosis, extensive research of the disease's atypical manifestations and potential treatment choices is required. As a result, the diagnosis is unusual and should be considered. Patients should feel reassured because the illness in itself is generally benign and has a good prognosis for treatment.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 23-year-old male from Uttar Pradesh
- **Chief Complaint:** Asymptomatic blue-black vascular lesion on the deltoid region of the right arm
- **History of Present Illness:** Lesion present for 8 years, measuring 2×1 cm; recurrent with spontaneous remission every 2-3 months. No progression in size, structure, or color. No pain, itching, or tenderness associated with the lesion.
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** No chronic infections or addictions
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Blue-black central vascular lesion surrounded by a red ecchymotic ring, confined to the deltoid without similar lesions elsewhere
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Dermoscopy: Central blue-black vascular region surrounded by red homogenous and vascular structures.
  - Excisional biopsy: Dilated blood vessels lined by hobnail epithelial cells, hemosiderin deposition in the dermis.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Differential Diagnoses Based on Clinical Presentation:**
  - Insect bite, considered but unlikely due to chronicity and recurrent nature.
  - Dermatofibroma, excluded due to absence of firm, brown papule with characteristic features.
  - Infantile hemangioma or tufted angioma, excluded due to lack of progression and developmental changes.
  - Melanocytic lesions, excluded after dermoscopy and absence of pigment network or malignancy features.
  - Kaposi sarcoma, considered but excluded due to lack of systemic or aggressive features (e.g., multifocality or rapid progression).
  
- **Histological Differential Diagnoses Based on Biopsy:**
  - Early Kaposi sarcoma and well-differentiated angiosarcoma, excluded due to benign histology and absence of malignancy.
  - Epithelioid hemangioma, progressive lymphangioma, and other vascular lesions considered but ruled out after observing hobnail endothelial cells, hemosiderin deposition, and non-infiltrative benign pattern.

- The stepwise exclusion of malignant, systemic, and inflammatory conditions confirmed consistency with a diagnosis of targetoid hemosiderotic hemangioma (THH).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Targetoid hemosiderotic hemangioma (THH) based on recurrent blue-black vascular lesion with red ecchymotic ring, distinct dermoscopic findings, and histopathological confirmation including hobnail endothelial cells and hemosiderin deposition. The absence of malignancy, other systemic conditions, or progression further supports the diagnosis.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 23-year-old male from Uttar Pradesh
- **Chief Complaint:** Asymptomatic blue-black vascular lesion on the deltoid region of the right arm
- **History of Present Illness:** Lesion present for 8 years, measuring 2×1 cm; recurrent with spontaneous remission every 2-3 months. No progression in size, structure, or color. No pain, itching, or tenderness associated with the lesion.
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** No chronic infections or addictions
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Blue-black central vascular lesion surrounded by a red ecchymotic ring, confined to the deltoid without similar lesions elsewhere
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Dermoscopy: Central blue-black vascular region surrounded by red homogenous and vascular structures.
  - Excisional biopsy: Dilated blood vessels lined by hobnail epithelial cells, hemosiderin deposition in the dermis.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 23-year-old male from Uttar Pradesh with a blue-black vascular lesion on his right deltoid that's been there for 8 years. It recurs every 2-3 months but doesn't seem to be getting bigger or changing in structure or color. No pain or itching. The physical exam shows a central blue-black vascular area with a red ecchymotic ring. Dermoscopy confirms the vascular structures, and the biopsy shows dilated blood vessels with hobnail epithelial cells and hemosiderin in the dermis.

First, I need to consider the differential diagnoses for vascular lesions. The key points here are the chronic nature, recurrent remission, and the histological findings. The lesion has been present for 8 years, which is a long time, and it's not progressing. The fact that it recurs but doesn't progress might suggest a benign condition. 

The presence of hobnail epithelial cells in the biopsy is a clue. I remember that in some vascular tumors, like hemangiomas, there can be specific cellular features. But hobnail cells are more commonly associated with certain types of angiomatous lesions. Wait, there's a condition called pyogenic granuloma, but those are usually more acute and might bleed more. However, the biopsy here shows hemosiderin, which is iron from old blood, indicating chronic hemorrhage. 

Another possibility is a vascular malformation. The lesion's stability over 8 years and the absence of growth might point towards a malformation rather than a tumor. Vascular malformations can be present at birth or appear later, and they can have periods of activity and quiescence. 

The dermoscopy findings of a central blue-black vascular region with surrounding red structures are consistent with some types of vascular malformations. The red ecchymotic ring might be due to hemosiderin deposition from previous episodes of bleeding or leakage. 

The histology mentions dilated blood vessels lined by hobnail epithelial cells. Hobnail cells are typically seen in conditions like pyogenic granuloma, but those are usually more inflamed and have a more pronounced inflammatory component. However, the presence of hemosiderin suggests chronicity. 

Wait, another thought: a congenital hemangioma. These can be present at birth or appear later, and they can regress over time. However, the patient is 23, and the lesion has been there for 8 years, so maybe it's an infantile hemangioma that has regressed but recurs? But infantile hemangiomas typically have a proliferative phase followed by involution. However, the recurrent nature here is a bit different. 

Alternatively, a venous malformation. Venous malformations can have a bluish appearance and may have a red ring due to hemosiderin. They can be present for a long time and may have periods of activity. The histology of a venous malformation would show dilated vascular channels, but the hobnail cells are a bit confusing here. 

Wait, the hobnail epithelial cells lining the vessels. That's a key point. In some cases, like in a hemangioma, the endothelial cells can be plump and have a hobnail appearance. But in a venous malformation, the vessels are usually more irregular and not lined by such cells. 

Another angle: the lesion is in the deltoid region, which is a common site for some vascular anomalies. The fact that it's localized and not spreading is a good sign for a benign process. 

Putting it all together: the chronic, stable lesion with recurrent remission, the dermoscopy findings, and the biopsy showing dilated vessels with hobnail cells and hemosiderin. The most likely diagnosis here is a hemangioma, specifically a cavernous hemangioma. However, the presence of hobnail cells might make me think of a more specific type. Alternatively, a pyogenic granuloma, but those are usually more inflamed and may not have such a long duration. 

Wait, but the biopsy mentions "hobnail epithelial cells" lining the vessels. Hobnail cells are a feature of some types of angiomatous lesions. In the context of a vascular lesion with hemosiderin, it's possible that this is a hemangioma with chronic hemorrhage. 

Another possibility is a blue nevus, but that's a melanocytic lesion, not vascular. The vascular component here is more prominent. 

Considering all the information, the most likely diagnosis is a hemangioma, specifically a cavernous hemangioma, which can present with these features. However, the presence of hobnail cells might be more indicative of a pyogenic granuloma, but the chronic and non-progressive nature makes that less likely. 

Alternatively, a venous malformation with some atypical features. But the histology with hobnail cells is more suggestive of a hemangioma. 

I think the best fit here is a cavernous hemangioma with chronic hemosiderin deposition, given the clinical and histological findings. The recurrent remission could be due to fluctuations in the vascular engorgement or spontaneous resolution of some components.
</think>

### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a long-standing, asymptomatic vascular lesion on the deltoid, characterized by a blue-black central region and a surrounding red ecchymotic ring. The lesion has remained stable in size and structure for 8 years, with recurrent episodes of remission. This chronic, non-progressive course suggests a benign vascular anomaly rather than a malignant or rapidly evolving condition.

<step 2> Dermoscopy reveals a central vascular component with surrounding red structures, consistent with vascular malformations or hemangiomas. The presence of hemosiderin deposition in the dermis on biopsy indicates chronic hemorrhage or leakage, which aligns with a long-standing vascular lesion.

<step 3> The excisional biopsy findings of dilated blood vessels lined by hobnail epithelial cells are critical. Hobnail cells are a hallmark of endothelial proliferation seen in hemangiomas, particularly cavernous hemangiomas. The combination of vascular dilation, hemosiderin, and hobnail endothelial cells strongly points to a hemangioma rather than a malformation or other vascular tumor.

<step 4> While venous malformations can present with similar clinical features, they typically lack the endothelial proliferation and hobnail cell morphology seen here. Pyogenic granuloma is less likely due to the chronic, non-progressive nature and absence of acute inflammation. The absence of systemic symptoms or family history further supports a localized, benign process.

<step 5> Given the histological confirmation of endothelial proliferation with hobnail cells, the diagnosis of a cavernous hemangioma with chronic hemosiderin deposition is most consistent with the clinical, dermoscopic, and pathological findings.

### Answer:
Cavernous Hemangioma with Chronic Hemosiderin Deposition
